metadata.title: The growing partisan politicization of non-political online spaces: A mixed-method analysis of news app reviews on Google Play between 2009 and 2022

abstract: Drawing on theories of identity politics and partisan polarization, we explored the politicization of Google Play's news app reviews-an explicitly non-political domain. Using a mixed-methods approach, Analysis of Topic Model Networks (ANTMNs), combining topic modeling, network analysis, community detection, and theory-driven qualitative reading, we analyzed 759,143 reviews from 2009 to 2022 across 46 news apps. Three themes emerged: Technical, Content Quality, and Political. The political discourse in reviews has intensified over the years, with notable spikes around election periods. Accusations of bias were found to correlate most strongly with lower app ratings. The findings provide alarming empirical evidence for the politicization of nonpolitical spaces, such as the app reviews section on app stores. With identity politics on the rise, this study sheds light on the importance of considering non-political online spaces for the study of political discourse.

sections.paper_id: Wang et al. - 2025 - The growing partisan politicization of non-political online spaces A mixed-method analysis of news
paragraphs: In recent years, scholars have expressed growing concerns about partisan divisions among Americans (Radwell and Israel, 2021), metastasized beyond lines of ideology, into domains of race, geography, and religion (Mason, 2018). As these schisms have become increasingly associated with the two main political parties (i.e. political sorting), many aspects that were once considered non-political have become politicized. From mask-wearing during coronavirus disease (COVID)-19 (Shin et al., 2022) and vaccination (Walter et al., 2020) to dating (Huber and Malhotra, 2017) and food preferences (Keefe, 2020), Americans' behaviors could now be effectively predicted from their identification as either Republicans or Democrats.

Political polarization has influenced Americans' media consumption as well. Up until the 1980s, Americans of all political affiliations have gotten most of their news from the three big networks (ABC, CBS, and NBC), several local or national newspapers, or the mainstream CNN (Napoli, 2020). With the rise of AM talk-radio, cable television networks, the Internet, and social media, it became substantially simpler for people to surround themselves with like-minded media (Jamieson and Cappella, 2008;Mitchell et al., 2021). By the 2010s, however, citizens could easily select and choose available news outlets and programs tailored to their tastes and prior political beliefs (Peterson et al., 2021). Following these developments, scholars have warned that the selection of like-minded news content and avoidance of incongruent views (Mutz, 2006) could lead to the adoption of more extreme views, which, in turn, could further increase selective exposure (Jamieson and Cappella, 2008). These processes could result in a cycle of polarization (Levendusky, 2013).

Considering these processes, we investigate how news consumers evaluate and discuss news outlets, with a particular attention to increasing trends in partisan politicization. We analyze 759,143 reviews published in reference to 46 news applications (apps) on Google Play, the biggest app store in the world, serving Android users. Notably, the app reviews section of Google Play is not designed for consumers to comment on the political nature of apps. Instead, the section is explicitly described by Google as a space for users to provide technical feedback to app developers. According to the "Ratings and Reviews on Google Play" page (Google and Play, 2023), reviews should address issues related to user experience and technical issues, for developers to consume and act upon. In fact, according to Google play, "solicitations, political or social commentary," which are not relevant to the "core of the consumer experience" should be considered off-topic. It is important to note that the Apple Store does not have such explicit guidelines regarding the content of app reviews. This difference makes the Google Play platform more aligned with our study's focus and theoretical framework, as it provides a clearer distinction between reviews that adhere to the intended purpose of the platform and those that deviate into political or social commentary. Despite Google's intentions, we anticipated a growing politicization of news app reviews. This expectation is based on observations made by other scholars in other traditionally non-political areas such as medicine (Ophir et al., 2023) and science (Walter et al., 2022). Given the scope of the data and the lack of prior research into similar phenomena, we approach these questions using an inductive mixed-method framework, the Analysis of Topic Model Networks (ANTMNs), combining unsupervised machine learning and in-depth manual coding (Walter and Ophir, 2019).

sections.paper_id: Wang et al. - 2025 - The growing partisan politicization of non-political online spaces A mixed-method analysis of news
sections.header_raw: Identity politics, mega-identities, and the politicization of everyday life
sections.header_norm: identity politics mega identities and the politicization of everyday life
paragraphs: The emotional and cognitive attachment to political parties has become omnipresent in American politics since the 1960s (Iyengar and Westwood, 2015). As argued by Mason (2018), Americans did not necessarily become more extreme in policy preferences. Instead, cultural and social preferences and traits have become significantly more "sorted" into two increasingly homogeneous party bins. Ideologically, the Republican party has become consistently more conservative, and to a much lesser degree, the Democratic more liberal (as evident, for example, in Pew Research data), 1 making it harder for politicians to reach agreements and cooperation across the aisle (Van Boven et al., 2018). Importantly, this political sorting extends beyond ideology, but rather spilled over to race, gender, age, income, education, and culture. As evident in myriad studies, Republicans and Democrats now drink different types of coffee, shop in different stores, and consume different art and music (Young, 2019). Democrats are more likely to shop at the likes of H&M, Starbucks, and Target, while Republicans prefer Walmart, Chick-fil-A, and Hobby Lobby (Lee, 2020).

Moreover, political identities are increasingly intertwined with the choice of news and social media platforms, with liberals consuming a more diverse and balanced media diet, whereas conservatives relying heavily on a few sources they trust, despite studies showing them to be biased and prone to misinformation (Benkler et al., 2018;Kor-Sins, 2023). Following decades of concerted efforts to undermine the mainstream media and other official sources of knowledge, conservatives and Republicans are now highly suspicious of mainstream media (Bedingfield, 2018;Jamieson and Cappella, 2018), and are more likely to isolate themselves in echo chambers (Levendusky et al., 2023).

Finally, sorting has made political parties a more prominent part of Americans' social identity (Turner, 2010), with politics being perceived as a zero-sum-game. The myriad consequences of sorting on anything from policy-change to lifestyle choices, what scholars have dubbed meta-identities, has been well-documented in the political science literature (Finkel et al., 2020).

sections.paper_id: Wang et al. - 2025 - The growing partisan politicization of non-political online spaces A mixed-method analysis of news
sections.header_raw: Blurring the lines between political and non-political online spaces
sections.header_norm: blurring the lines between political and non political online spaces
paragraphs: Google's request for users to avoid politics in reviews (Google Play, 2023) begs the question of what should be considered political in the first place. As Mutz (2001) suggested, defining politics has long been a daunting task, one that is becoming increasingly harder to demarcate in today's climate of politicization and polarization. Feminist scholars, for example, have famously argued that the term "political" should not be limited to electoral issues, but needs to be expanded to broader aspects of everyday life affected by power relations (Hanisch, 1969;Suk et al., 2023). Due to our focus on partisan identities, we follow conceptualizations of politicization as the signaling or communication of collective identities that stand in opposition to each other, expressed in discourse of us versus them (Bakardjieva, 2015), often associated with moralistic language of right versus wrong (Mouffe, 2005). When studying the bipartisan American system (Mason, 2018), we are particularly interested in the Republican/conservative versus Democrat/ liberal dichotomy. Our definition is not limited to explicit expressions of electoral preferences (Riedl et al., 2023), but rather to any communication that interprets events, people, or social phenomena in ways that project one's association with or disdain toward one of the two political parties and its followers (Sunstein, 2018).

Empirical evidence has supported a concerning growth in partisan politicization and polarization of non-political online spaces. Content analyses of online non-political chat rooms and blogs, for example, had revealed the existence of a substantial number of political discussions (Munson and Resnick, 2011;Wojcieszak and Mutz, 2009). A study combining content analysis and survey data on a popular, entertainment-oriented, subreddit focused on The Bachelor television show found a large number of political discussions as well, with users often expressing intolerance toward those with opposing political views (Chen and McCabe, 2024). Another study demonstrated that nearly half of the political discussions on Reddit are coming from non-political subreddits (Rajadesingan et al., 2021). We argue that the app review section could serve as one more vehicle for expression of political sectarianism, making it another tool for the formation of public discourse and opinion (Neubaum and Krämer, 2017), political perceptions (Gunther, 1998;Lee and Tandoc, 2017), and political persuasion (Winter et al., 2015).

In light of the documented increase in distrust in media (Masullo et al., 2023), especially among conservatives (Levendusky et al., 2023) and the tendency of highly-partisan individuals to be more engaged with political expression (Bail, 2022;Mutz, 2006), we expect politicized reviews to be dominated by those perceiving some or all of the media to be biased against them and hostile to their views. Rojas (2010) suggested that individuals who perceive others as disproportionately affected by media, or view media as hostile to their side, are more likely to adopt new communication technologies to amplify their views. We therefore expect the politicization of news app reviews to be characterized by increased selectivity, hostility, and expression of political identity, all potential accelerators of polarization.

sections.paper_id: Wang et al. - 2025 - The growing partisan politicization of non-political online spaces A mixed-method analysis of news
sections.header_raw: Selectivity, hostility, and media consumption as a political identity
sections.header_norm: selectivity hostility and media consumption as a political identity
paragraphs: Today's high-choice media environment offers many options for political and partisan consumers. Studies have shown that when given the choice, individuals tend to consume news that align with their preexisting political views (Mitchell et al., 2021;Stroud, 2008). This self-selection into congruent media can reinforce one's prior beliefs and strengthen a sense of belonging to a particular social identity (Young, 2019), which could intensify polarization and extremism (Mason, 2018). Scholars have been warning that selective exposure to like-minded information can create echo chambers and reduce tolerance for opposing viewpoints (Jamieson and Cappella, 2008), with more recent data demonstrating an asymmetry, where echo chambers, while rare on average, are more common on the American right (Levendusky et al., 2023). The relationship between polarization and selective exposure exhibits a reciprocal and reinforcing dynamic.

Partisans tend to selectively engage with media that mirror their political leanings, a process that in turn can amplify their endorsement of more extreme views (Iyengar et al., 2019;Stroud, 2010). Over time, this cyclic process can further heighten polarization (Rhodes, 2022).
In light of the above, our investigation into news app reviews on Google Play leads us to pose the first research questions:
RQ1. What are the predominant themes present in news app reviews on Google Play?
Given evidence for increased partisan polarization and emerging politicization of non-political online spaces, we hypothesize that:
H1. The prominence of politicized discourse in news app reviews on Google Play increased from 2009 to 2022.

With the rise in prominence of news media in general (Napoli, 2020) and partisan outlets specifically (Peck, 2019), mainstream news media institutions and journalists have faced growing scrutiny and criticism from politicians, pundits, and commentators (Ladd, 2011). The heightened attention to news channels and the positioning of journalists by conservatives as political actors (Carlson et al., 2021) have shaped public perceptions of journalistic professionalism and objectivity (Tsfati and Cappella, 2003). Studies have shown that common public perceptions, especially among conservatives, of a general liberal bias in news coverage were not a mere reflection of the content of news, but rather a response to frequent attacks from the political elites (Watts et al., 1999), and particularly conservatives and Republicans who for decades have practiced media-bashing as a political performance (Jamieson and Cappella, 2008). Former Republican President Donald Trump has further amplified these trends with relentless attacks and delegitimization campaigns that portrayed journalists using populist terms such as the enemy of the people and fake news media (Carlson et al., 2021). The increase in accusations of bias was accompanied by a dramatic increase in attacks on journalists both offline and online since 2015 (Wells et al., 2020).

Studies on the Hostile Media Bias (HMB) had consistently shown that perceptions of bias are subjective and can be influenced by political ideology (Hassell et al., 2020). Studies have demonstrated that partisans on both sides of arguments and ideological disputes tend to perceive neutral and balanced news articles as biased against their side (Vallone et al., 1985). The effect of HMB was particularly large when neutral articles were presented as being written by partisan outlets that were inconsistent with participants' political beliefs (Reid, 2012). As a result, while conservatives and Republicans are, on average, more likely to accuse the media of biases, such perceptions can also arise among liberals (e.g. Stroud, 2010). Recognizing that allegations of media bias, especially when examining multiple news apps across the political spectrum, can originate from both partisan sides, we ask the following research question: RQ2. Does the prominence of politicized topics and themes vary across news app reviews for media outlets with different political leanings? Specifically, given the effects of elites' rhetoric on public news trust and perceptions of media hostility (Van Duyn and Collier, 2019;Watts et al., 1999), we are particularly interested in content concerning bias criticism, hostility, and sentiment. Therefore, we ask:
RQ3. Does the political leaning of a news app correlated with the amount of bias criticism, hostility, and the sentiment used to write about them?

Finally, a key affordance of the app review section is the star rating system, which could potentially serve as a proxy for public opinion, and could, in turn, influence other users' consumption decisions (Kim et al., 2018). Audience feedback in general (Lee and Tandoc, 2017), and numeric ratings, in particular (Broersma, 2019), provide users with a proxy for the quality of products and public reaction to them (Lee and Tandoc, 2017). Since we believe politicization could be used to both endorse or criticize a news outlet (i.e. a very critical political comment is likely to be accompanied by a negative rating, but a review that praises the political view of the app would likely be accompanied by a positive score), we suggest a research question to examine the relationship between politicized language and numeric ratings of reviews, controlling for other coded factors, including hostility, bias criticism, and political leaning: RQ4. How does the utilization of politicized language influence the numeric rating for news apps.

In addition, users have the option to upvote or downvote reviews, signaling their endorsement of or disagreement with a review (notice, the upvote is for the review itself, not the app). We expect politicization to appeal to some users but to be seen as a distraction or inappropriate in this context by others. We therefore pose the relationship between politicized language and upvoting as a research question and not a directional hypothesis:
RQ5. What is the relationship between politicization and the number of upvotes an app has received?
sections.text:
Today's high-choice media environment offers many options for political and partisan consumers. Studies have shown that when given the choice, individuals tend to consume news that align with their preexisting political views (Mitchell et al., 2021;Stroud, 2008). This self-selection into congruent media can reinforce one's prior beliefs and strengthen a sense of belonging to a particular social identity (Young, 2019), which could intensify polarization and extremism (Mason, 2018). Scholars have been warning that selective exposure to like-minded information can create echo chambers and reduce tolerance for opposing viewpoints (Jamieson and Cappella, 2008), with more recent data demonstrating an asymmetry, where echo chambers, while rare on average, are more common on the American right (Levendusky et al., 2023). The relationship between polarization and selective exposure exhibits a reciprocal and reinforcing dynamic.

Partisans tend to selectively engage with media that mirror their political leanings, a process that in turn can amplify their endorsement of more extreme views (Iyengar et al., 2019;Stroud, 2010). Over time, this cyclic process can further heighten polarization (Rhodes, 2022).

In light of the above, our investigation into news app reviews on Google Play leads us to pose the first research questions:

RQ1. What are the predominant themes present in news app reviews on Google Play?

Given evidence for increased partisan polarization and emerging politicization of non-political online spaces, we hypothesize that:

Former Republican President Donald Trump has further amplified these trends with relentless attacks and delegitimization campaigns that portrayed journalists using populist terms such as the enemy of the people and fake news media (Carlson et al., 2021). The increase in accusations of bias was accompanied by a dramatic increase in attacks on journalists both offline and online since 2015 (Wells et al., 2020).

Audience feedback in general (Lee and Tandoc, 2017), and numeric ratings, in particular (Broersma, 2019), provide users with a proxy for the quality of products and public reaction to them (Lee and Tandoc, 2017). Since we believe politicization could be used to both endorse or criticize a news outlet (i.e. a very critical political comment is likely to be accompanied by a negative rating, but a review that praises the political view of the app would likely be accompanied by a positive score), we suggest a research question to examine the relationship between politicized language and numeric ratings of reviews, controlling for other coded factors, including hostility, bias criticism, and political leaning: RQ4. sections.paper_id: Wang et al. - 2025 - The growing partisan politicization of non-political online spaces A mixed-method analysis of news
Wang et al. - 2025 - The growing partisan politicization of non-political online spaces A mixed-method analysis of news
paragraphs: Google Play reviews were scraped on 13 September 2022 for each news app posted between 2009 and 2022 using Python's Beautiful Soup module that parses web pages and provides a convenient interface for navigating content. The Google Play store was officially launched in 2012, yet some of the apps were previously available via the Android store, and are still available for scraping, allowing us to go back to 2009 in our data (McIlroy et al., 2016).

We compiled a list of 46 news media outlets based on the criteria of appearing in both the Google Play store on the date of data collection, and in the political leaning classification provided by the AllSides media bias Chart, v7 from 2022 (https:// www.allsides.com/media-bias/media-bias-chart). While no categorization into political slant is without flaws, AllSides was used in prior research for annotating the political leaning of news media (e.g. Matsumura et al., 2024;Rozado et al., 2022;Spinde et al., 2021). Nevertheless, mindful of the potential biases inherent in any categorization, we conducted a sensitivity analysis. This involves comparing the AllSides categorization with other sources, a process thoroughly detailed in Supplemental Appendix A. This comparison provided reassurance that our results are not significantly influenced by our choice of categorization method. Furthermore, while minor discrepancies in the degree of political bias (e.g. whether an outlet should be considered "leaning left" versus "left") were noted, there was a substantial agreement among various categorizations regarding the overall political leanings of the new outlets we studied. We therefore collapsed "leaning left" and "left" into "leaning left" and "leaning right" and "right" into "leaning right." This adjustment suits the theoretical framework of our study.

In total, we collected 759,143 reviews. As shown in Table 1, on average, left-leaning media outlets received the greatest number of reviews, followed by moderate, and then right media outlets. Our corpus consisted of 25 left-leaning, 7 centrist, and 14 rightleaning apps. Only very few disagreements were noted when comparing AllSides with other popular categorizations (see Supplemental Appendix A), providing us more confidence as to the stability of our analysis.

sections.paper_id: Wang et al. - 2025 - The growing partisan politicization of non-political online spaces A mixed-method analysis of news
paragraphs: Thematic content. Our inductive analysis of topics and themes relied on the ANTMN framework (Walter and Ophir, 2019), which consists of four methodological steps: topic modeling, topic networking, community detection, and in-depth qualitative analysis. Topic modeling, in our case using Latent Dirichlet Allocation, is an unsupervised machine learning method used to identify themes in large textual data (Blei, 2003). Topics are distribution lists of words and their probabilities based on co-occurrences of words in the same documents (here, Google Play reviews). The number of topics and the alpha hyperparameter (which accounts for the short length of the reviews) were chosen carefully based on a five-fold run over different combinations of κ (number of topics, between 5 and 100, in skips of 5) and α (0.01-0.5), after which we chose the best performing model using perplexity scores (see Ophir et al., 2023). While modeling in ANTMN is data-driven and is conducted inductively, the qualitative analysis of the top words and documents associated with each topic and their corresponding themes (clusters) is guided by the theoretical framework described earlier. The relative prominence of themes was also examined over time, providing a window into politicization dynamics. The optimal model, based on perplexity scores, had 35 topics with 2/35 alpha hyperparameter. For details of model selection, see Supplemental Appendix B.

In the second step of ANTMN, we calculated a network of topics based on their co-occurrence in the same reviews. This was done by computing the cosine similarity over the document topic matrix and using these similarities as links in the network. Each topic was treated as a node, and their co-occurrence formed the links. To reduce network density and discard non-significant links, we applied a backbone method (Serrano et al., 2009). In the third step, we used the Louvain community detection algorithm to group the topics into broader themes. Finally, we performed a qualitative analysis of the model and its output, and labeled each theme based on our close reading.

Manual coding. To gain additional insight into the nature of politicization, the three authors collaboratively developed a codebook based on the existing literature and then tested and refined it using small samples of reviews coded by two independent coders. The final analysis included sentiment of the review (positive vs negative), accusation of journalistic bias, and expressions of hostility toward journalists/media outlets. Three rounds of training, totaling 180 reviews, were conducted. The training samples were not used for the final analysis. The coders reached an adequate reliability for all variables in the last round of training (see Table 2). One exception was hostility, where we believe that the relatively lower reliability resulted from the rarity of the category in the corpus (i.e. a zero-inflated case). We thus recommend reading into the analysis of this specific variable with some caution. After reaching reliability, the two coders manually coded a random sample of 542 reviews.

sections.paper_id: Wang et al. - 2025 - The growing partisan politicization of non-political online spaces A mixed-method analysis of news
Wang et al. - 2025 - The growing partisan politicization of non-political online spaces A mixed-method analysis of news
paragraphs: First, we analyzed the dominant themes in the corpus of Google Play reviews (RQ1). As shown in Figure 1, the model identified 35 topics that were clustered into three broad themes. Based on our in-depth qualitative analysis of each topic and their corresponding clusters, we labeled the themes Content (blue), Technical (orange), and Political (pink).

The Political theme consisted of topics such as political bias in news coverage, aimed at other users and the journalists themselves, the Content theme focused on the quality of journalism, and the Technical theme focused on issues related to user experience, aimed at the development team. The top 10 unique words and texts per each topic can be found in the Supplemental Appendix C.

To gain additional confidence in our modeling and analyses, we further validated the inductive approach by comparing human and automated coding results, revealing that reviews marked as political by human coders contained more than double the politicized language as detected by the algorithm. Notably, non-political reviews still featured some political language, reflecting the inherent complexity of topic modeling. More information can be found in Supplemental Appendix D.

In line with the study's aims, our analysis primarily concerns the Political theme. We identified topics related to bias accusations, political discussions, and evaluation of the factuality of media outlets, shows, and hosts. For example, in the Factual versus Biased Outlets topic, reviewers often expressed the reasoning behind their selective exposure to specific outlets. For example, and in line with prior research showing asymmetry in distrust in mainstream media, a conservative user argued that "the Epoch Times is one of the ONLY real news stations today! You can always count on them to give us the facts . . ." and another said "thank God for Fox News the only station that will report the actual news, not like the liberal media ABC NBC CBS MSNBC CNN News only Fox News is trusted."

As expected, some topics were dedicated to explicitly blame incongruent outlets of being biased or fake, such as the biased and fake, political bias, and political grievances topics. Reviews on these topics often referred to specific political events or politicians. For instance, on the biased and fake topic, a review attacked CNN, stating that where President Trump is concerned, CNN makes biased, false, and half-true reports, in order to further its own agenda of delegitimizing him, and to create, based upon these false and inflammatory reports, anger and fear in the minds of our citizens. This is not news, it's propaganda of the worst kind.

Similarly, another review criticized CNN's coverage of the 2020 election, saying, "swamp media, I am a Republican but this network is fake news just like the others and I am sure owned and controlled by the same people Terrible news. How they cover the 2020 election and post-election is criminal."

Users who used the political bias topic often compared their favorite channel to what they perceived as inferior others. One post highlighted that compared with AP and NPR, Fox News catered to narrow sub-populations only, saying that the information Fox uses for their content is selectively chosen to appeal to the conservative right-leaning audience. They don't discuss things objectively and let inherent bias and emotions devalue their content. This is not a good news source for people of this country, while sources like AP news and NPR offer much better objective journalism and reporting.

Another review accused Yahoo! News of distorting political facts toward a liberal perspective: "Yahoo has joined hands with other liberal media and instead of just reporting political facts, distort the facts to promote a liberal agenda. First, we analyzed the dominant themes in the corpus of Google Play reviews (RQ1). As shown in Figure 1, the model identified 35 topics that were clustered into three broad themes. Based on our in-depth qualitative analysis of each topic and their corresponding clusters, we labeled the themes Content (blue), Technical (orange), and Political (pink).

The Political theme consisted of topics such as political bias in news coverage, aimed at other users and the journalists themselves, the Content theme focused on the quality of journalism, and the Technical theme focused on issues related to user experience, aimed at the development team. The top 10 unique words and texts per each topic can be found in the Supplemental Appendix C.

To gain additional confidence in our modeling and analyses, we further validated the inductive approach by comparing human and automated coding results, revealing that reviews marked as political by human coders contained more than double the politicized language as detected by the algorithm. Notably, non-political reviews still featured some political language, reflecting the inherent complexity of topic modeling. More information can be found in Supplemental Appendix D.

In line with the study's aims, our analysis primarily concerns the Political theme. We identified topics related to bias accusations, political discussions, and evaluation of the factuality of media outlets, shows, and hosts. For example, in the Factual versus Biased Outlets topic, reviewers often expressed the reasoning behind their selective exposure to specific outlets. For example, and in line with prior research showing asymmetry in distrust in mainstream media, a conservative user argued that "the Epoch Times is one of the ONLY real news stations today! You can always count on them to give us the facts . . ." and another said "thank God for Fox News the only station that will report the actual news, not like the liberal media ABC NBC CBS MSNBC CNN News only Fox News is trusted."

As expected, some topics were dedicated to explicitly blame incongruent outlets of being biased or fake, such as the biased and fake, political bias, and political grievances topics. Reviews on these topics often referred to specific political events or politicians. For instance, on the biased and fake topic, a review attacked CNN, stating that where President Trump is concerned, CNN makes biased, false, and half-true reports, in order to further its own agenda of delegitimizing him, and to create, based upon these false and inflammatory reports, anger and fear in the minds of our citizens. This is not news, it's propaganda of the worst kind.

Similarly, another review criticized CNN's coverage of the 2020 election, saying, "swamp media, I am a Republican but this network is fake news just like the others and I am sure owned and controlled by the same people Terrible news. How they cover the 2020 election and post-election is criminal."

paragraphs: new media & society 27( 8) grow in mid-2015, around the time Donald Trump announced his presidential candidacy. Two substantial peaks can also be seen around the 2016 and 2020 elections.

To gain additional insight into the news politicization, we analyzed topics marked by hostility toward media: biased and fake and political grievances. These topics, centered around allegations of political bias, provided insights into shifts in users' perceptions toward news content. As shown in Figure 3, both topics demonstrated upward trends over time. The political grievances topic notably surged around the January 6th Capitol riot, when supporters of Donald Trump violently invaded the Capitol building following Donald Trump's refusal to accept his defeat in the 2020 presidential election, which was based on misinformation and conspiracy theories (Eggers et al., 2021). We postulate that these unsubstantiated claims for voter fraud and the violent events that followed likely contributed to the increased prevalence of politically charged reviews in this period.

In terms of frequency, we observe spikes in activity around specific dates, which can largely be explained by 2016 and 2020 US presidential elections. One of the most significant spikes in activity was recorded in July 2016 when Donald Trump secured the necessary delegates to officially become the Republican Party's nominee for the US presidency. Another notable spike was in October 2020, during which key events such as the first presidential debate, President Trump's COVID-19 response, and the confirmation hearings for Supreme Court nominee Amy Coney Barrett.

sections.header_raw: Politicization across different media outlets categories
sections.header_norm: politicization across different media outlets categories
paragraphs: RQ2 examined whether reviews for media outlets of different political slant exhibited different levels of politicization. An analysis of variance of manual-coding reviews (n = 542) was conducted to examine the relationship between political slant and share of politicized language in reviews. The results showed a significant relationship between the political leaning of media outlets and political posts, F(2,238) = 5.931, p = .003. Further analysis using Tukey's post hoc test indicated that right-leaning media received the highest share of politicized language in reviews (M = 0.458, SD = 0.256) followed by left-leaning media (M = 0.340, SD = 0.277, p = .001), and centrist media (M = 0.289, SD = 0.237, p = .000). The difference between centrist and right-leaning media was significant (p = .006), as was the difference between right-and left-leaning media (p = .011). The difference in politicization between left-leaning and centrist media was not significant (p = .537). Sentiment, perceived bias, and media hostility RQ3 probed differences in criticism, hostility, and sentiment among reviews for right, left, and centrist news apps. We relied on the manual coding to investigate the sentiment, perceived bias and media hostility. Ignoring posts centered on technical aspects or unrelated to politics, our sample of 542 reviews revealed 45% contained political content. Of these, 172 had positive sentiments, 66 were negative, 8 neutral, 21.54% accused apps of bias, and 10.57% showed media hostility. Probit regressions indicated that there were no significant differences across political alignments (all p > .05).

RQ4 asked whether the star-system rating (1-5) was affected by the political leanings of media outlets, prevalence of the Political theme, expressions of hostility toward media and journalist, and accusations of bias (sentiment was not used as a predictor to avoid a tautology). Regression results in Table 3 revealed that using right-leaning news outlets as the comparison category, left-leaning (p = .96) and centrist news outlets (p = .96) did not have significant impact on star ratings. However, biased criticism (p < .00) and media hostility (p = .00) negatively impacted star ratings, with bias criticism playing a major role. There was no significant impact of Political theme on star ratings (p = .116).

Finally, RQ5 asked the relationship between political reviews and the number of upvotes. Given that most reviews had few upvotes (Median = 0, M = 2.424, Max = 2745), we used a zero-inflated Poisson regression to analyze the data. Results indicated that more political reviews were less likely to get upvotes from other users (b = -0.722).

sections.paper_id: Wang et al. - 2025 - The growing partisan politicization of non-political online spaces A mixed-method analysis of news
paragraphs: Driven by identity politics, Americans have increasingly aligned themselves with two homogeneous groups-conservative Republicans or liberal Democrats (Mason, 2018). This identity sorting has resulted in spillovers of polarization and politicization into nonpolitical (at least in the partisan sense) spaces, from health and science to interpersonal relationships and entertainment. In this study, we offer a novel approach to studying politicization of non-political spaces. Relying on a mixed-method approach that combining inductive unsupervised machine learning methods and deductive, theory-driven qualitative analysis (Walter and Ophir, 2019), we investigated whether the user-generated news app reviews on Google Play, which is intended for technical and user-experience discussions, has become more politicized and polarized from 2009 to 2022. Our findings provide alarming evidence for a rapidly growing politicization of the ostensibly non-political space of reviews in Google Play. We identified 35 topics that were automatically clustered into three themes using the ANTMN approach (Walter and Ophir, 2019), which we qualitatively labeled Political, Content, and Technical. While the Technical theme consisted of topics in which users approach developers with complaints and suggestions have to do with the user experience of the app itself (as intended by Google Play's rules), the Political theme was loaded with discussions of journalistic quality, and particularly political bias. The Content theme was preoccupied with the quality of journalism, but was not necessarily about politics, and rarely included direct attacks on the integrity of media.

The Content and Political themes were often used in the same reviews, while the technical discussions were usually separated. Nevertheless, our method was able to isolate the Political theme, enabling us to focus our analysis on this explicitly politicized language cluster. These analyses, in turn, demonstrated that over the years, the share of reviews' language associated with politics increased dramatically. Some scholars have argued that in the absence of clear institutional slants, non-political online spaces could be expected to provide an environment for citizens to engage in everyday political talk, and facilitate more diverse and deliberative conversations (Wojcieszak and Mutz, 2009;Yan et al., 2018). However, our findings revealed the existence of politicized reviews that tended to focus on political bias and political grievances. These results suggest that within the context of journalism, political discussions in non-political spaces may also veer toward polarization, as individual identities can be triggered by the media brands themselves as part of broader culture wars.

Various factors may have contributed to this trend. Distrust in mainstream media (asymmetrically higher among conservatives, see Benkler et al., 2018), was often fueled by the attacks from political figures, especially conservatives and Republicans (Jamieson and Cappella, 2008), and by media polarization (Klar and Krupnikov, 2016;Levendusky and Malhotra, 2016). Studies have demonstrated that the growing polarization in America has been driven more by affect than by substantial changes in policy support, expressed via a growing dislike and intolerance of the opposite party (Krupnikov and Ryan, 2022). We expected the politicization of non-political spaces (e.g. Ophir et al., 2023) to make its way to news apps reviews as well, based on prior research showing news consumers who actively follow politics are also more likely to express their political opinions (Iyengar et al., 2019) and strong partisans being more vocal online than moderates (Bail, 2022).

Interestingly, in addition to the linear trend toward politicization, we also observed temporary peaks in Political theme during significant political event, such as presidential elections. These spikes could possibly be explained by the agenda-setting theory (McCombs and Shaw, 1972), and in the case of reviews could perhaps be thought of as examples of a unique type of inter-media agenda setting processes (Harder et al., 2017). Furthermore, exposure to politicized reviews might influence public opinion and perceptions (Gunther, 1998), which could in turn potentially amplify perceived polarization.

We found politicization to be specifically high when users reviewed non-centrist outlets. Given identity-driven selective exposure (Peterson et al., 2021) and media outlets' inclination to pick sides (Ladd, 2011), it is unsurprising to see partisan outlets receiving more politicized reviews than centrist media, which at least claim to represent both sides. It should be noted that we did not directly observe selective exposure, or exposure in general. One may leave a review on CNN's app decrying them to be "fake news" without ever using the app or reading any of its journalistic content. We do, however, speculate that the politicization of app reviews may lead to selective news consumption, which could, potentially, bolster polarization. Future studies may look more closely at the relationship between reviews and actual consumption using complementary methods.

In our analysis, we did not find significant differences in hostility, sentiment, or bias accusations across various political news outlets. Content factors, however, impacted app ratings, which were found in prior studies to be key drivers of users' behavior (Chen, 2017;Margolin and Markowitz, 2018). Our results showed that while the political leanings of media outlets did not sway star ratings in our data, one prominent Political theme did negatively correlate with them. Users often discussed politics to convey criticism or negative sentiments. Notably, reviews highlighting bias or hostility were associated with lower ratings. As our data indicate a rise over time in accusations of bias, these could have long-term effects on news consumption and polarization. Driven by identity politics, Americans have increasingly aligned themselves with two homogeneous groups-conservative Republicans or liberal Democrats (Mason, 2018). This identity sorting has resulted in spillovers of polarization and politicization into nonpolitical (at least in the partisan sense) spaces, from health and science to interpersonal relationships and entertainment. In this study, we offer a novel approach to studying politicization of non-political spaces. Relying on a mixed-method approach that combining inductive unsupervised machine learning methods and deductive, theory-driven qualitative analysis (Walter and Ophir, 2019), we investigated whether the user-generated news app reviews on Google Play, which is intended for technical and user-experience discussions, has become more politicized and polarized from 2009 to 2022. Our findings provide alarming evidence for a rapidly growing politicization of the ostensibly non-political space of reviews in Google Play. We identified 35 topics that were automatically clustered into three themes using the ANTMN approach (Walter and Ophir, 2019), which we qualitatively labeled Political, Content, and Technical. While the Technical theme consisted of topics in which users approach developers with complaints and suggestions have to do with the user experience of the app itself (as intended by Google Play's rules), the Political theme was loaded with discussions of journalistic quality, and particularly political bias. The Content theme was preoccupied with the quality of journalism, but was not necessarily about politics, and rarely included direct attacks on the integrity of media.

The Content and Political themes were often used in the same reviews, while the technical discussions were usually separated. Nevertheless, our method was able to isolate the Political theme, enabling us to focus our analysis on this explicitly politicized language cluster. These analyses, in turn, demonstrated that over the years, the share of reviews' language associated with politics increased dramatically. Some scholars have argued that in the absence of clear institutional slants, non-political online spaces could be expected to provide an environment for citizens to engage in everyday political talk, and facilitate more diverse and deliberative conversations (Wojcieszak and Mutz, 2009;Yan et al., 2018). However, our findings revealed the existence of politicized reviews that tended to focus on political bias and political grievances. These results suggest that within the context of journalism, political discussions in non-political spaces may also veer toward polarization, as individual identities can be triggered by the media brands themselves as part of broader culture wars.

Various factors may have contributed to this trend. Distrust in mainstream media (asymmetrically higher among conservatives, see Benkler et al., 2018), was often fueled by the attacks from political figures, especially conservatives and Republicans (Jamieson and Cappella, 2008), and by media polarization (Klar and Krupnikov, 2016;Levendusky and Malhotra, 2016). Studies have demonstrated that the growing polarization in America has been driven more by affect than by substantial changes in policy support, expressed via a growing dislike and intolerance of the opposite party (Krupnikov and Ryan, 2022). We expected the politicization of non-political spaces (e.g. Ophir et al., 2023) to make its way to news apps reviews as well, based on prior research showing news consumers who actively follow politics are also more likely to express their political opinions (Iyengar et al., 2019) and strong partisans being more vocal online than moderates (Bail, 2022).

Interestingly, in addition to the linear trend toward politicization, we also observed temporary peaks in Political theme during significant political event, such as presidential elections. These spikes could possibly be explained by the agenda-setting theory (McCombs and Shaw, 1972), and in the case of reviews could perhaps be thought of as examples of a unique type of inter-media agenda setting processes (Harder et al., 2017). Furthermore, exposure to politicized reviews might influence public opinion and perceptions (Gunther, 1998), which could in turn potentially amplify perceived polarization.

We found politicization to be specifically high when users reviewed non-centrist outlets. Given identity-driven selective exposure (Peterson et al., 2021) and media outlets' inclination to pick sides (Ladd, 2011), it is unsurprising to see partisan outlets receiving more politicized reviews than centrist media, which at least claim to represent both sides. It should be noted that we did not directly observe selective exposure, or exposure in general. One may leave a review on CNN's app decrying them to be "fake news" without ever using the app or reading any of its journalistic content. We do, however, speculate that the politicization of app reviews may lead to selective news consumption, which could, potentially, bolster polarization. Future studies may look more closely at the relationship between reviews and actual consumption using complementary methods.

In our analysis, we did not find significant differences in hostility, sentiment, or bias accusations across various political news outlets. Content factors, however, impacted app ratings, which were found in prior studies to be key drivers of users' behavior (Chen, 2017;Margolin and Markowitz, 2018). Our results showed that while the political leanings of media outlets did not sway star ratings in our data, one prominent Political theme did negatively correlate with them. Users often discussed politics to convey criticism or negative sentiments. Notably, reviews highlighting bias or hostility were associated with lower ratings. As our data indicate a rise over time in accusations of bias, these could have long-term effects on news consumption and polarization. paragraphs: PhD is an Assistant Professor of Communication at the University at Buffalo. His work combines computational methods for text mining, network analysis, experiments, and surveys to study media content and effects in the areas of political, science, and health communication. He is the head of the Media Effects, Misinformation, and Extremism lab and a member of the Center for Information Integrity at the University at Buffalo.
